evidence_quotes.quote: There is a growing problem in the gaming industry where consumers purchase games at full price, expecting lasting access, only to have those games suddenly rendered unplayablewith no prior warning or explanation.

paragraphs.text: Were currently witnessing a deeply concerning development: payment processors are beginning to deny services to platforms such as Steam, Itch.io, and others that distribute and sell digital content As a result, many creators are being stripped of their ability to earn a living, simply because theyre unable to sell their work. Consumers, too, are being affectedsome are losing access to purchased content or even having their accounts shut down altogether. These platforms already enforce strict policies and actively remove anything that may contain illegal material. Despite this, the pressure has led to sweeping restrictions that impact all adult digital products, not just problematic ones. This is a troubling example of censorship and disproportionate influence. Legally produced adult content should be accessible to adults who wish to purchase it, and creators have every right to distribute and profit from their work. Allowing payment processors to dictate what people can or cannot buywhen no laws are being brokensets a dangerous precedent. Another important issue currently gaining attention is the Stop Killing Games movement. Id like to urge you to consider incorporating video game preservation into the Digital Fairness Act. There is a growing problem in the gaming industry where consumers purchase games at full price, expecting lasting access, only to have those games suddenly rendered unplayablewith no prior warning or explanation. This practice is not only unfair to buyers but also highlights a broader need for clear standards around the longevity and accessibility of digital products. Preserving video games as part of digital consumer rights would help ensure that people retain access to the products theyve legally purchased and prevent the erasure of entire works from cultural and entertainment history.
